prompt:
I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. He's hoping to get into a competitive writing program with something like this. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Grade: 7/10

This is a **solid, competent essay** with genuine strengths, but it falls a bit short of what competitive writing programs typically seek. Here's my breakdown:

## What Works Well

- **Clear narrative arc** with good pacing and natural voice (reads like a real person, not forced)
- **Honest vulnerability** — he doesn't make himself look good unfairly
- **Specific details** that ground the story (the "pick and roll," "stay out of the way," Marcus making the team)
- **Unexpected pivot** to debate shows real reflection and self-knowledge
- **Sound mechanics** — no major technical issues

## What Holds It Back

- **Underdeveloped emotional moment** — The scrimmage is his lowest point but gets only a paragraph. More sensory detail and specificity here would strengthen the piece
- **Generic reflection** — "failure is just information" is wise but somewhat predictable. The insights feel a bit *told* rather than *discovered* through the narrative
- **The debate transition needs work** — *Why* debate specifically? What was the spark? This pivot is interesting but too rushed to feel earned
- **Plays it safe emotionally** — He moves from upset to acceptance fairly smoothly. Real standout essays often sit longer in the discomfort
- **Filler language** — "Obviously," some summary statements that don't add anything

## Bottom Line

For a general assignment or college application to a mid-tier school, this would be strong. But for a *competitive writing program*, admissions committees see hundreds of honest, reflective essays like this. The student needs to either:
- Go deeper into specific moments (show, don't tell)
- Find a more surprising or original insight
- Take more emotional/intellectual risks

It's a good foundation—he just needs to push further.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works Well

**Strong narrative fundamentals**: The essay has clear structure, good pacing, and specific details that ground the story (the "pick and roll" moment, the kid saying "stay out of the way," Friday's list). These concrete moments make it feel genuine.

**Authentic voice and honesty**: This sounds like a real person reflecting, not someone trying to sound impressive. The student admits they didn't try again, that they were genuinely out of their depth—that's refreshingly candid.

**Thoughtful reflection**: The three lessons are actually earned, not grafted on. The insight that "you don't know what you don't know" is genuinely useful, and the pivot to debate as a better fit feels organic rather than like forced redemption.

## What Holds It Back

**Prose lacks sophistication**: For a competitive writing program, this reads more like a strong high school essay than polished writing. Sentences are functional but plain. There's repetition ("pretty good," "pretty quickly," "pretty much") and minimal sentence variety. The vocabulary is conversational rather than precise.

**Tells rather than shows**: The student states "I was really upset" but doesn't dramatize it through action or sensory detail. Competitive writing programs expect you to *render* emotion through concrete detail, not announce it.

**Somewhat familiar territory**: Basketball tryout failure is a common essay topic. The execution is honest, but the subject matter itself isn't distinctive enough to compensate for prose that's just competent rather than compelling.

**The ending feels a bit flat**: "So yeah, I failed..." is charming but slightly too casual for a conclusion. And the final insight, while true, is stated in pretty generic terms.

## Bottom Line

This student can clearly write and tell a story with integrity. For a college application essay, it's solid. **But for a *competitive writing program specifically*—where they're evaluating your prose quality, not just your story—this would need more polish.** The narrative is there; the writing needs to be more precise, more vivid, and less reliant on common phrasing.

**Suggestion**: Have them revise by cutting unnecessary words, varying sentence structure, and replacing at least one scene with more sensory/dramatic detail. That could push it to an 8.

## What Works

**Strong narrative foundation.** The essay has clear structure, genuine vulnerability, and a real moment of failure. The detail about Marcus making the team while the student didn't creates effective tension, and the pivot to debate feels earned.

**Good self-awareness.** The student doesn't make excuses or wallow—he acknowledges his overconfidence and the gap between casual play and competitive reality. That's mature.

What did his legs feel like when the faster kids blew past him? Where was the coach standing? One vivid scene beats pages of summary.

**Minimal dialogue and scene-building.** Only one line of actual dialogue ("just stay out of the way"), and it's reported rather than placed in a moment. Competitive programs want writers who can reconstruct scenes, not just report on them.

**The reflection feels familiar.** "Failure is just information" and "you don't know what you don't know" are real insights, but they land as conclusions rather than hard-won discoveries. The writing would be stronger if we *felt* how he arrived there.

**The debate pivot is underdeveloped.** It's mentioned but not explored. Why debate specifically? How did that decision happen? This could be a second scene rather than a throwaway detail.

**Generic closing.** "It worked out fine" actually undercuts the emotional weight. A competitive program would want him to sit with the complexity longer.

## For Your Student

This is a solid, safe essay that shows competence. For a competitive program, they'll want him to take more risks with language, linger in specific moments, and trust readers to find meaning in vivid detail rather than spelling it out. The reflection is good, but the *writing craft* needs to match that insight.

prompt:
I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. He's hoping to get into a competitive writing program with something like this. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

prompt:
I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. He's hoping to get into a competitive writing program with something like this. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. He's hoping to get into a competitive writing program with something like this. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

**Safe prose.** Phrases like "So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts" and "it sounds like a weird pivot" feel like you're backing away from the moment. The writing is clear but doesn't have any distinctive voice or stylistic flair.

**Flat ending.** "Failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information" is wise, but it's close to a greeting card. The last lines don't quite land with the punch they could.

## To Strengthen It

- **Dig deeper into one moment** rather than covering everything quickly. Maybe spend real time on seeing that list, or on your first debate competition, rather than rushing through.
- **Find the specific, unusual detail** that only *you* would notice or remember—something that distinguishes this from other failure essays.

---
